metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in the design that the git checkout + rsync + chown steps run whenever `changed BOT_PATHS OR changed SECRETS_PATHS` (not just BOT_PATHS), so that $DEPLOY_REPO always holds the TARGET fetch-secrets.sh before it is invoked.
evidence: Plan: 'Placement: AFTER the rsync+chown (so $DEPLOY_REPO holds the TARGET fetch-secrets.sh)'
Plan: 'Guard the existing review-bot redeploy block on: changed BOT_PATHS OR changed SECRETS_PATHS'
autodeploy.sh lines 183-192: git checkout + rsync + chown are INSIDE the `if changed "$BOT_PATHS"` block — they do not currently run for any other condition
If SECRETS_PATHS changes but BOT_PATHS does not, rsync hasn't run, so $DEPLOY_REPO/infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh is the previously-deployed (old) version
findings.finding: The plan's 'guard the existing review-bot redeploy block on BOT_PATHS OR SECRETS_PATHS' is ambiguous about whether rsync runs for a SECRETS_PATHS-only change. If rsync remains gated solely on BOT_PATHS, a secrets-only commit (e.g., fetch-secrets.sh gains a new get_param call) would invoke the OLD $DEPLOY_REPO/infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh — missing the new SSM read and defeating the SECRETS_PATHS trigger entirely. The plan's placement note ('AFTER the rsync+chown so $DEPLOY_REPO holds the TARGET fetch-secrets.sh') implicitly requires rsync to run on SECRETS_PATHS changes too, but this dependency is not stated explicitly.
findings.location: Remaining work (AC2) — 'Precise design' section; Placement bullet and Guard bullet
scenarios: A commit adds a new SSM parameter to fetch-secrets.sh (new get_param call) but touches no BOT_PATHS file — autodeploy fires on SECRETS_PATHS, but invokes the old fetch-secrets.sh which lacks the new get_param call, so the new env var is still absent from .env after the deploy.
A commit modifies only ssm.tf (e.g., adds a comment or new param declaration) — SECRETS_PATHS fires, rsync hasn't run, $DEPLOY_REPO fetch-secrets.sh may be stale from a prior deploy.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Precise design section, add a bullet: 'The git checkout, rsync, and chown steps are moved outside/before the BOT_PATHS-only guard and run whenever changed BOT_PATHS OR changed SECRETS_PATHS — this ensures $DEPLOY_REPO always reflects the TARGET commit's fetch-secrets.sh before it is invoked. The :prev tag, build, and compose up remain gated on the same OR condition.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether rsync (and the prerequisite `git checkout $TARGET` in $MIRROR_DIR) runs for a SECRETS_PATHS-only change, since the plan's placement rationale ('AFTER rsync so $DEPLOY_REPO holds the TARGET fetch-secrets.sh') is only correct if rsync is explicitly part of the secrets-only branch too.
evidence: Plan: 'Placement: AFTER the rsync+chown (so $DEPLOY_REPO holds the TARGET fetch-secrets.sh)'
Plan: 'Guard the existing review-bot redeploy block on: changed BOT_PATHS OR changed SECRETS_PATHS (so a secrets-only change still recreates the container to pick up the new .env)'

autodeploy.sh lines 183-192: rsync is inside the 'if changed "$BOT_PATHS"' block — widening the guard to OR SECRETS_PATHS without also specifying that rsync runs for the new branch means rsync may be skipped when only SECRETS_PATHS changed
ABSENCE: the plan never explicitly resolves whether rsync (and hence git checkout of TARGET into $MIRROR_DIR) runs for a secrets-only-changed deploy
findings.finding: The plan specifies that fetch-secrets must be invoked from $DEPLOY_REPO 'AFTER the rsync+chown (so $DEPLOY_REPO holds the TARGET fetch-secrets.sh)', but it never specifies whether rsync runs for a SECRETS_PATHS-only change (BOT_PATHS unchanged). The existing redeploy block — whose guard the plan widens to 'BOT_PATHS OR SECRETS_PATHS' — contains the rsync call internally. If a secrets-only commit triggers the block but rsync is not executed (because BOT_PATHS didn't change), $DEPLOY_REPO/infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh may be stale, directly contradicting the plan's own stated placement rationale.
findings.location: Remaining work / Precise design — 'Placement: AFTER the rsync+chown' and 'Guard the existing review-bot redeploy block on: changed BOT_PATHS OR changed SECRETS_PATHS'
scenarios: A commit adds a new SSM key to fetch-secrets.sh only (no BOT_PATHS file touched). The new guard fires the redeploy block. If rsync is not run, $DEPLOY_REPO still contains the old fetch-secrets.sh, which does not emit the new key, silently failing the feature AC2 is meant to fix.

An implementor interprets 'guard the existing redeploy block' as 'widen the if-condition but leave the body unchanged' — rsync and git checkout remain BOT_PATHS-only, causing the stale-script problem.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to 'Precise design': 'For a SECRETS_PATHS-only change, rsync must also run (same checkout+rsync block as BOT_PATHS) so that $DEPLOY_REPO/infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh is the TARGET version before it is invoked. The rsync step is therefore conditional on (BOT_PATHS OR SECRETS_PATHS changed), not BOT_PATHS alone.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test case covering: fetch-secrets runs before compose up on a BOT_PATHS-only change (no SECRETS_PATHS files touched), verifying the OR guard wires both branches correctly.
evidence: Test (a) specifies only: 'fetch-secrets runs before compose up on a secrets-only change'
The design states the guard is 'changed BOT_PATHS OR changed SECRETS_PATHS', meaning the BOT_PATHS branch also now runs fetch-secrets — a changed behavior
No test covers 'fetch-secrets runs before compose up on a BOT_PATHS-only change'
findings.finding: The BOT_PATHS-triggered redeploy path is CHANGED by this plan (fetch-secrets is now inserted before 'docker compose up' in that branch too), but no test is specified for that changed path — only the 'secrets-only change' scenario is covered. A regression in the BOT_PATHS branch (e.g., fetch-secrets not running, running at the wrong point, or the OR guard being mis-wired) would not be caught.
findings.location: AC / Tests section — item (a); 'Precise design' section — 'Guard the existing review-bot redeploy block on: changed BOT_PATHS OR changed SECRETS_PATHS'
scenarios: A BOT_PATHS-only deploy (e.g. bot code change, no secrets file touched) silently skips fetch-secrets due to a mis-wired OR condition — the suite stays green but the live path is broken
findings.suggested_fix: Add test (c): simulate a change touching only BOT_PATHS (e.g. a bot source file), assert fetch-secrets is invoked before 'docker compose build && up', and assert the deploy completes successfully. This can mirror the structure of test (a) with a different changed-files fixture.

evidence: file_impact is empty
findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Wrap the fetch-secrets.sh invocation with a timeout (e.g., `timeout 60 bash ...`) so a hung SSM call is treated as a failure and triggers the existing fail-safe (err marker + backoff + exit 1) rather than blocking indefinitely.
evidence: Plan specifies 'fetch-secrets is fail-fast — on any SSM error it exits 1' but does not address the hung/no-response case
A network partition to SSM can produce an indefinite hang rather than an exit code
No 'timeout <N>' wrapper or equivalent is mentioned in the invocation spec
findings.finding: No timeout is specified for the fetch-secrets.sh invocation inside autodeploy.sh; if SSM hangs (network partition, service degradation without a clean error), the deploy blocks indefinitely rather than aborting fail-safe.
findings.location: Remaining work / Precise design — 'Invoke ENV_FILE=... bash fetch-secrets.sh'
scenarios: SSM endpoint is unreachable (VPC misconfiguration, AWS outage) and the AWS CLI hangs waiting for a response — autodeploy.sh blocks indefinitely, preventing all future deploy ticks from running
A slow SSM response exceeds the autodeploy polling interval, causing deploys to queue or overlap
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the bare invocation with: `timeout 60 ENV_FILE="$COMPOSE_DIR/.env" bash "$DEPLOY_REPO/infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh" || { emit_err 'secrets-fetch-failed'; record_backoff_failure; exit 1; }`

findings.finding: AC3 ('File paths: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh, infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh, infra/terraform/ssm.tf') is a scope boundary listing, not a measurable acceptance criterion — it states no observable outcome and cannot be evaluated as 'pass/fail'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'File paths: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh, infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh, infra/terraform/ssm.tf'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the AC3 file-path line and replace it with a criterion such as: 'Running `terraform plan infra/terraform/ssm.tf` after apply shows no diff for the reviewbot-tickets-pat slot.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that exercises the new autodeploy fetch-secrets path end-to-end on the production box: trigger a deploy with a SECRETS_PATHS change and assert the new env var is present in the running reviewbot container (e.g. `docker exec reviewbot env | grep -F NEW_KEY=expected_value`), or document explicitly that this live check is deferred to a named follow-up with rationale.
evidence: AC4: 'Tests cover: (a) fetch-secrets runs before compose up on a secrets-only change; (b) fetch-secrets failure aborts the deploy without building.'
AC2: 'a new SSM-backed env key reaches the box on deploy without a manual boot' — no stated proving command that actually triggers a deploy and inspects the running container's env.
Per E6(f): 'a proving command that passes on a mock/offline substitute which BYPASSES the new behavior does NOT prove the criterion'
The plan contains no step that runs autodeploy.sh against the live box with a real SSM-backed key and confirms the env var is present in the running container post-deploy.
findings.finding: The new autodeploy code path — fetch-secrets.sh invoked by autodeploy.sh against live SSM before 'docker compose up' — has no acceptance criterion that exercises it end-to-end in production. AC4's tests (a) and (b) can be satisfied entirely with a mocked/stubbed fetch-secrets.sh, meaning they can be green while the live SSM integration path (real AWS creds, real parameter store call, real .env write) never executes and may silently fail on the box.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 and AC4
scenarios: Shell tests pass (fetch-secrets stubbed to exit 0) but on the real box the IAM role lacks ssm:GetParameter on the new path, causing fetch-secrets to exit 1 and autodeploy to abort every deploy — only discovered in the next production incident.
fetch-secrets.sh ENV_FILE argument handling changes silently; mocked tests still pass; live deploys write .env to the wrong path.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'AC5 [operator-attested]: After merging, trigger a canary deploy by touching infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh; confirm on the box that `docker exec reviewbot env | grep REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` returns the expected value and that no secrets-fetch-failed marker appears in autodeploy logs — record the deploy run-id as attestation.' This is operator-attested per ADR 0043, requires no new tooling, and closes the live-path verification gap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Test design' subsection to 'Remaining work' specifying the test framework (e.g. bats), file path (e.g. infra/scripts/tests/test_autodeploy.sh), and a brief description of the two test cases so AC4 maps to a concrete deliverable.
evidence: AC4: 'Tests cover: (a) fetch-secrets runs before compose up on a secrets-only change; (b) fetch-secrets failure aborts the deploy without building.'
ABSENCE: 'Remaining work / Precise design' describes only the production code change to autodeploy.sh; no test file path, framework, or structure is mentioned anywhere in the plan body.
findings.finding: AC4 specifies two test cases (fetch-secrets runs before compose up on a secrets-only change; fetch-secrets failure aborts the deploy without building) but the plan body ('Remaining work' / 'Precise design') contains no description of how or where these tests are to be implemented — no test framework, no file path, no test structure. AC4 is an orphan criterion with no covered deliverable in the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4; 'Remaining work' section (entirely absent)
scenarios: Implementer has a clear production design but no guidance on where to put tests, what framework to use (bats? pytest? shell?), or what the test harness looks like for a shell-script deploy pipeline — test criterion is unverifiable without a described deliverable.
findings.suggested_fix: Under 'Remaining work', add: 'Test design: Add bats tests in infra/scripts/tests/test_autodeploy.sh (or equivalent). Case (a): stub SECRETS_PATHS change with no BOT_PATHS change, assert fetch-secrets.sh is invoked before docker-compose-up stub. Case (b): have the fetch-secrets stub exit 1, assert the script exits before the build step and emits the secrets-fetch-failed marker.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief design note (AGENTS.md section or inline doc in autodeploy.sh) explaining the SECRETS_PATHS trigger pattern — what it covers, why it exists, and how to extend it when a new SSM-backed param is added.
evidence: Plan introduces a new pipeline stage (secrets refresh before compose up) and a new trigger class (SECRETS_PATHS) as architectural patterns
AC and file-paths list only infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh as changed; no mention of AGENTS.md, ADR, or design-doc update
'SECRETS_PATHS trigger' is a non-obvious convention that determines which file edits cause a secrets refresh — undocumented, it will be rediscovered the hard way
findings.finding: No documentation update (ADR, AGENTS.md, or design doc) is planned for the new 'conditional secrets-refresh pipeline stage' pattern and the SECRETS_PATHS trigger concept — a novel architectural decision that future operators and contributors need to understand.
findings.location: Remaining work / Precise design section; AC file paths list
scenarios: A future contributor adds a new SSM-backed param but doesn't know to add the relevant file to SECRETS_PATHS, silently breaking the auto-refresh guarantee
An on-call engineer debugging a missed .env update has no design reference explaining why SECRETS_PATHS exists or how to extend it
findings.suggested_fix: Add a comment block in autodeploy.sh above the SECRETS_PATHS declaration explaining: 'Add a file here when it governs a new SSM-backed env key; changes to these files trigger fetch-secrets.sh before compose up.' Optionally add a one-liner to AGENTS.md cross-referencing this convention.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (or amend fetch-secrets.sh to use) atomic .env write semantics — write to a temp file in the same directory, then `mv` (rename) to `.env` — so a mid-write crash/signal leaves the prior `.env` fully intact, making the fail-safe guarantee unconditional.
evidence: Plan states the safety guarantee: 'exits 1 WITHOUT touching .env' on SSM error
Plan also states fetch-secrets.sh is 'referenced/triggering, unchanged' — its write mechanism is not described
The guarantee covers SSM-error exit but is silent on mid-write crash atomicity
Shared state (.env) is consumed by the review-bot container on every compose-up
findings.finding: The plan asserts that fetch-secrets.sh 'exits 1 WITHOUT touching .env' on any SSM error, and uses this as the foundation of the fail-safe guarantee — but the plan does not confirm whether fetch-secrets.sh writes .env atomically (temp-file + rename) or in-place (truncate then write). If the script uses in-place writes, a process signal or OOM kill arriving mid-write produces a half-written .env that is silently corrupt, violating the stated guarantee even though no SSM error occurred.
findings.location: Remaining work / Error contract section: 'fetch-secrets is fail-fast — on any SSM error it exits 1 WITHOUT touching .env'
scenarios: SIGKILL arrives while fetch-secrets.sh is mid-write to .env (in-place mode): .env is partially overwritten, next compose-up launches bot with a malformed/empty env, causing silent misconfiguration rather than a clean abort
Disk-full condition causes a partial write to .env with no SSM error: plan's fail-safe does not catch this path
findings.suggested_fix: In fetch-secrets.sh, write all secrets to a temp file (e.g., `ENV_FILE_TMP="${ENV_FILE}.tmp.$$"`) and only `mv "$ENV_FILE_TMP" "$ENV_FILE"` after all SSM fetches succeed. On any error, `rm -f "$ENV_FILE_TMP"` and exit 1. This makes the write atomic from the filesystem's perspective and removes the partial-write risk.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the 'Precise design' section, explicitly reference the existing OBS_PATHS/CERTBOT_PATHS re-materialization precedent and record the rationale for why the SECRETS_PATHS block uses a fatal error contract (abort + backoff) rather than the non-fatal pattern those blocks use.
evidence: Plan: 'On that failure, emit an err secrets-fetch-failed marker, record_backoff_failure, and exit 1 — abort the deploy BEFORE tagging :prev or building'
autodeploy.sh line 228–237: OBS_PATHS failure path emits err obs-materialize-failed but does NOT call record_backoff_failure or exit 1 — deploy continues
autodeploy.sh line 251–259: CERTBOT_PATHS failure path similarly non-fatal
ABSENCE: plan never references OBS_PATHS/CERTBOT_PATHS precedent or explains the fatal/non-fatal divergence
findings.finding: The plan's 'Precise design' section presents the SECRETS_PATHS block as a standalone design without acknowledging the directly analogous OBS_PATHS and CERTBOT_PATHS blocks already in autodeploy.sh (lines 228–259). Those blocks use a non-fatal error contract (emit err marker, continue deploy); the new SECRETS_PATHS block uses a fatal error contract (abort + backoff). The plan does not record the rationale for this asymmetry relative to the codebase's own established pattern, leaving the implementer without a grounded justification for why secrets-fetch failure is fatal while obs/certbot-materialize failure is non-fatal.
findings.location: Remaining work / Precise design — error contract paragraph; autodeploy.sh lines 228–259 (OBS_PATHS/CERTBOT_PATHS blocks)
scenarios: An implementer following the OBS_PATHS/CERTBOT_PATHS precedent might accidentally implement the SECRETS_PATHS block as non-fatal (matching the established pattern), silently building against a stale .env — exactly the defect AC2 is meant to fix.
A future reviewer of autodeploy.sh sees three path-triggered blocks with two different error contracts and no inline rationale, creating maintenance confusion about the intended invariant.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'Unlike the OBS_PATHS and CERTBOT_PATHS blocks (non-fatal: a stale probe/timer is observable but does not degrade the gate), a stale or missing .env would silently break the bot's SSM-backed features — so fetch-secrets failure is fatal: abort before build, record_backoff_failure, exit 1. The running bot stays on its current image; no rollback is needed because nothing was built.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or explicit blocking note specifying when the least-privilege PAT swap must occur (e.g., 'must be completed before next PAT rotation cycle' or 'blocker on the follow-up ticket'), or document the compensating control (token expiry date, repo-scope audit) that bounds the risk window while the broad PAT is live.
evidence: Plan quote: 'Least-privilege PAT swap (broad classic repo-scope -> fine-grained contents:write only): human-mintable only; recommended, tracked separately.'
AC1 is marked DONE in prod — the broad-scoped PAT is already operational.
ssm.tf line 49: /rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat slot active and in use.
T10(b) requires roles/policies scoped to minimum; the PAT has broader-than-minimum repo access with no inline compensating control (e.g., IP restriction, expiry enforcement) specified in this plan.
findings.finding: The plan activates the REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT — a 'broad classic repo-scope' GitHub PAT — without any compensating IAM/scope control in this plan's scope; the minimum-privilege swap to a fine-grained contents:write-only token is explicitly deferred with no blocking gate.
findings.location: Out of scope / follow-ups: 'Least-privilege PAT swap (broad classic repo-scope -> fine-grained contents:write only): human-mintable only; recommended, tracked separately.'
scenarios: If the PAT is compromised (e.g., via the review-bot container), an attacker has broad repo-scope access across all repositories the token covers, not just the tickets repo.
The plan ships and the follow-up ticket is deprioritized, leaving the broad-scoped credential live indefinitely.
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's Out of scope section or as a new AC, add: 'The broad classic repo-scoped PAT must be rotated to a fine-grained contents:write-only token within [N days / before [date]]; a reminder or ticket dependency is set.' Alternatively, document that the PAT has a short expiry date set and the fine-grained swap will occur at next rotation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before or alongside AC2 shipping, confirm the provisioned `reviewbot-tickets-pat` SSM value is a fine-grained PAT scoped to `contents:write` on the tickets repo only (not a classic broad-scope token); if it is not, either block AC2 on rotation or explicitly re-scope this follow-up to a time-bound SLA rather than open-ended 'tracked separately'.
evidence: Plan out-of-scope section: 'Least-privilege PAT swap (broad classic repo-scope -> fine-grained contents:write only): human-mintable only; recommended, tracked separately.'

infra/terraform/ssm.tf line 47-49: 'A fine-grained GitHub PAT with contents:write on the tickets repo ONLY' — the IaC comment asserts fine-grained scope, but the plan's out-of-scope note implies the operator-provisioned value may not yet meet this standard.

The plan's new code (autodeploy.sh invoking fetch-secrets.sh before every qualifying compose up) actively reads and re-materializes this credential into the running container .env, making the plan a direct consumer of the over-scoped credential — not merely adjacent to it.
fetch-secrets.sh line 91: 'reviewbot_tickets_pat="$(get_param_optional reviewbot-tickets-pat)"' — credential is fetched and written to .env unconditionally once populated.
findings.finding: The plan's new autodeploy path actively propagates `reviewbot-tickets-pat` into the container .env on every qualifying deploy, yet the plan explicitly acknowledges the PAT currently provisioned in prod may hold a broad classic repo-scope grant rather than the fine-grained `contents:write`-only scope it requires, and defers the least-privilege fix to a separate follow-up.
findings.location: Out of scope / follow-ups — 'Least-privilege PAT swap (broad classic repo-scope -> fine-grained contents:write only): human-mintable only; recommended, tracked separately.'
scenarios: If the provisioned PAT holds classic repo-scope (access to all repos the token owner can reach), a compromise of the review-bot container or the .env file grants broader repository write access than the feature requires.
The newly automated deploy path (AC2) ensures the broad-scope credential reaches the container on every qualifying deploy without a manual operator step, increasing the attack surface window.
findings.suggested_fix: Promote the least-privilege PAT swap from 'out of scope / tracked separately' to a time-bound follow-up with an explicit SLA (e.g., 'complete before the next quarter boundary' or 'block the next unrelated deploy if still classic-scope'). If the swap truly cannot be done now (human-mintable only), add a runtime guard in fetch-secrets.sh or a deployment gate that checks the token's scope class (GitHub API `GET /user` with the token returns `X-OAuth-Scopes`; a non-empty `repo` scope is a signal the token is classic) and emits an AUTODEPLOY err marker rather than silently propagating an over-scoped credential.

findings.finding: The proposed env-delivery fix ('autodeploy re-runs fetch-secrets before compose up') is mechanically broken for the exact scenario the plan names: a new SSM param added with NO source-code change. In autodeploy.sh, fetch-secrets would only be invoked inside the `if changed "$BOT_PATHS"` block (the only place `docker compose up -d` is called). A new env key (e.g. a future REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT being newly populated, or any future SSM-backed key) with no corresponding change to BOT_PATHS would never trigger that block, leaving the .env stale exactly as before — violating AC2 ('New env keys reach the box on deploy without a manual boot').
findings.location: What section — 'Design fix for env delivery on deploy (e.g. autodeploy re-runs fetch-secrets before compose up)'; AC: 'New env keys reach the box on deploy without a manual boot'

findings.finding: The 'design fix for env delivery on deploy' is specified only as 'e.g. autodeploy re-runs fetch-secrets before compose up' — the placement within autodeploy.sh's existing flow is ambiguous (before rsync? after rsync? only on a BOT_PATHS change? always?). autodeploy.sh explicitly excludes .env from rsync (line 88) and has no fetch-secrets call anywhere in its flow; adding one without specifying WHERE in the decision tree it fires (and under what condition) leaves the implementation underspecified as a protocol co-location gap.
findings.location: What section: 'Design fix for env delivery on deploy (e.g. autodeploy re-runs fetch-secrets before compose up)'; File: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh

findings.finding: The plan says 'Apply terraform (ssm.tf slot /rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat)' as if the slot must be created, but ssm.tf already contains that parameter in rebar_secret_params (line 49) with lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] } — terraform apply is a no-op for this slot's value. The operative step is 'aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite ... --value <PAT>'; if the implementer assumes terraform apply populates the PAT, the REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT will remain empty/CHANGEME and fetch-secrets will silently yield a blank value (get_param_optional never aborts).
findings.location: What section — 'Apply terraform (ssm.tf slot /rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat), populate the PAT'
findings.suggested_fix: Split into two named steps: (1) 'Confirm SSM slot exists (terraform apply is a no-op here — slot already in ssm.tf)'; (2) 'Populate the PAT: aws ssm put-parameter --name /rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat --type SecureString --overwrite --value <real-PAT>'.

findings.finding: The plan says 'Apply terraform (ssm.tf slot /rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat)' implying the SSM parameter slot needs to be created by terraform, but /rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat is already declared in ssm.tf (line 49) and exists in the rebar_secret_params locals block. The operative step is only populating its value out-of-band (aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite), not a terraform resource creation. The 'terraform apply' framing may cause confusion about what the apply actually does.
findings.location: What section: 'Apply terraform (ssm.tf slot /rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Apply terraform (ssm.tf slot /rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat)' with 'Populate the existing SSM slot /rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat (already declared in ssm.tf) via aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite; terraform apply is not needed for this slot.'

findings.finding: The plan adds a fetch-secrets.sh invocation inside autodeploy.sh (changing existing deploy behavior) with no stated rollback path or failure-handling strategy. If fetch-secrets.sh fails at deploy time (SSM unreachable, IAM permission error, missing parameter, etc.), all future automated deploys could be blocked or silently degraded, with no documented back-out plan.
findings.location: What section — 'autodeploy re-runs fetch-secrets before compose up'; file infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh

findings.finding: Adding fetch-secrets.sh as a mandatory step inside autodeploy.sh introduces a hard SSM dependency into every deployment; no error handling, timeout, or fallback is specified for the case where SSM is unavailable or slow.
findings.location: ## What — 'autodeploy re-runs fetch-secrets before compose up'; infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh
findings.suggested_fix: In autodeploy.sh: `if ! timeout 15 ./fetch-secrets.sh; then echo '[autodeploy] WARNING: fetch-secrets failed, proceeding with existing .env' >&2; fi` — or hard-abort with `set -e` if stale env is unacceptable. Document the chosen policy in a comment.

findings.finding: The autodeploy fix (adding fetch-secrets.sh execution before compose up) is a behavior change affecting every future deploy, but no rollback path is specified — if fetch-secrets.sh hangs, errors, or has a credential gap, all subsequent deploys are broken with no stated recovery mechanism.
findings.location: ## What — 'Design fix for env delivery on deploy (e.g. autodeploy re-runs fetch-secrets before compose up)' / File: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh
findings.suggested_fix: In autodeploy.sh, run fetch-secrets.sh and gate on its exit code: if it fails, emit a clear error and either abort the deploy (fail-safe) or skip the secrets refresh and continue with the existing .env (fail-open), depending on desired policy. Document the revert as: remove/comment the fetch-secrets call to restore prior behavior.

findings.finding: The plan's activation sequence ('populate PAT, re-run fetch-secrets.sh, restart bot') omits a required step: if the tickets clone was previously deferred (REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT was absent at the last entrypoint run), restarting the bot with the PAT now set will trigger a fresh clone attempt — but the plan does not specify whether the operator must explicitly confirm that reviewbot-ensure-tickets.sh has converged the store (setting the .env-id marker) before declaring AC1 satisfied. The entrypoint silently swallows a ensure-tickets failure, so 'no emission failed warnings' can remain unverified without a specified check.
findings.location: What section: 'Apply terraform … re-run fetch-secrets.sh, restart bot; verify artifact emission works against the (now hand-created) gerrit_reviewbot_tickets volume'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' section: 'After restart, confirm store convergence: run `docker exec <review-bot-container> reviewbot-ensure-tickets.sh` and verify exit 0 + the final log line; then trigger a test review and confirm no ARTIFACT_EMIT_ERROR in journald.'

findings.finding: The plan proposes 'autodeploy re-runs fetch-secrets before compose up' as the env-delivery fix without evaluating the established path-triggered conditional re-materialization pattern already used for the same problem class in autodeploy.sh (OBS_PATHS/CERTBOT_PATHS blocks). Unconditional fetch-secrets re-runs would add 4+ SSM API calls and an IMDS round-trip to every 2-minute autodeploy tick — including no-op ticks where nothing changed — while the existing pattern gates re-materialization on changed() to avoid exactly this overhead.
findings.location: What section / 'Design fix for env delivery on deploy (e.g. autodeploy re-runs fetch-secrets before compose up)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a SECRETS_PATHS trigger (e.g. 'infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh infra/terraform/ssm.tf') and call fetch-secrets conditionally via `if changed "$SECRETS_PATHS"` — matching the OBS_PATHS/CERTBOT_PATHS pattern already in the script. Note that this still doesn't cover the case of an operator populating a NEW SSM value without any code change; document that edge case and handle it (e.g. a manual fetch-secrets re-run + compose restart) as an operator step.

findings.finding: The autodeploy .env delivery fix is under-specified: the plan says 'Design fix for env delivery on deploy (e.g. autodeploy re-runs fetch-secrets before compose up)' — the 'e.g.' and 'design' language leaves the concrete mechanism, invocation point, and failure handling unspecified. autodeploy.sh currently hard-excludes .env from rsync and never calls fetch-secrets.sh; a fetch-secrets.sh failure under set -euo pipefail would abort the entire deploy (including the bot rebuild), which is an unacceptable blast radius for an SSM transient.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section — 'Design fix for env delivery on deploy'; infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh RSYNC_EXCLUDES line 88
findings.suggested_fix: Add a targeted fetch-secrets.sh call in autodeploy.sh inside the `if changed "$BOT_PATHS"` block, BEFORE `docker compose up -d`, with a non-fatal error path (log + AUTODEPLOY_ERROR metric on SSM failure, but do not abort the deploy if the .env already exists — guard with `[ -f "$COMPOSE_DIR/.env" ]`). This preserves the fail-fast property on first boot while tolerating transient SSM unavailability on subsequent autodeploys.

findings.finding: The plan proposes injecting fetch-secrets.sh into the autodeploy path but does not specify the failure-mode interaction: fetch-secrets.sh is fail-fast (exit 1) on any required SSM param. If SSM is transiently unreachable during an autodeploy tick, this would block the deploy and trigger backoff — potentially freezing the fail-closed gate. The plan offers no precondition (e.g. non-fatal SSM fetch, or separate OPTIONAL-only re-fetch) to contain this new failure mode.
findings.location: What / 'Design fix for env delivery on deploy (e.g. autodeploy re-runs fetch-secrets before compose up)'
findings.suggested_fix: In autodeploy.sh, call a restricted variant of fetch-secrets that only refreshes OPTIONAL params (or wrap the call: `fetch-secrets.sh || { err fetch-secrets-failed "SSM unreachable during deploy; .env unchanged"; }` without `exit 1`) so a transient SSM outage degrades gracefully rather than blocking every deploy tick.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC4 the test framework (e.g. bats-core), the expected file path (e.g. `tests/bats/test_autodeploy.bats`), and the invocation command (e.g. `bats tests/bats/test_autodeploy.bats`) so the criterion is provable in-session without a design decision by the executor.
evidence: AC4: 'Tests cover: (a) fetch-secrets runs before compose up on a secrets-only change; (b) fetch-secrets failure aborts the deploy without building.' — no file path, no framework (bats, shunit2, pytest?), no run command stated.
Under E6: 'every completion-relevant claim has a concrete proving command/check that produces evidence on success.'
Under E2: no test framework is named; choosing 'an appropriate test framework' is a design decision deferred to the executor.
findings.finding: AC4 states 'Tests cover: (a) ... (b) ...' but does not specify the test framework, the file path where tests live, or the command to invoke them — leaving an executor unable to verify in-session that the tests exist and pass without stopping to make a framework/location decision.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4
scenarios: Executor writes tests in bats under tests/bats/autodeploy.bats; reviewer expects them in a different location/framework; PR is disputed on scope.
CI pipeline has no bats runner; executor must also wire CI, which is undescribed work.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC4: 'Run with: `bats tests/bats/test_autodeploy.bats` (add file if absent); tests must exit 0 in CI.' If a different framework is already in use for autodeploy.sh tests, name it explicitly.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
